title: The Dollar Milkshake Theory
speaker: Brent Johnson
contrarian_takes: Stablecoins cannibalizing Bitcoin in global south
quotable: Universal high income and social unrest. That's my prediction.
summary: Dollar Milkshake Theory: the USD will strengthen BECAUSE the global system is breaking down — all roads lead through the dollar. Foreign governments must buy dollars to service debt, creating a vortex. The dollar is the milkshake straw sucking up global capital. Structural dollar demand overwhelms all other factors including deficits and money printing.

title: Intent Engineering — From Prompting to Organizational AI
speaker: Enterprise AI Panel
contrarian_takes: Organizational alignment beats raw model capability
quotable: The distinction between AI that fails and AI that succeeds at the wrong thing is the most important unsolved problem in enterprise AI.
summary: From prompting to 'intent engineering' — CLA reported AI agent doing work of 853 FTEs. Shift from writing prompts to designing organizational AI: defining goals, constraints, and success criteria at the system level. The next evolution beyond prompt engineering.

title: AI, Energy, Robots & The Future
speaker: Elon Musk
contrarian_takes: Saving for retirement is pointless due to coming deflation
quotable: The limiting factor for the next two years will be the ability to turn chips on.
summary: Musk on AI timeline: Grok 5 may be 'nearly perfect' on hard benchmarks. Prediction: universal high income AND social unrest simultaneously. Future currency = wattage. China passing 3x US electricity output in 2026. Humanity capturing less than half-billionth of sun's energy — need to reach a millionth as minimum goal. Simulation theory: only interesting simulations survive. Healthcare will be transformed by AI within 5 years.

title: Miami Urbanism, Housing & Infrastructure
speaker: David Martin
contrarian_takes: Market-based zoning reform over government subsidies
quotable: We need to improve the salaries of our teachers.
summary: David Martin (Terra) on Miami's transformation: infrastructure investment thesis, housing strategy, mixed-use development. Miami becoming a tech hub requires solving housing, transit, and urban density. Real estate as long-term infrastructure play.

title: Decentralized Finance, AI & The Internet for Money
speaker: Jeff Yan
contrarian_takes: Centralized systems move faster but decentralized wins long-term
quotable: I hope Hyperliquid becomes the internet for money.
summary: Hyperliquid founder on building DeFi infrastructure: decentralized perpetual futures exchange processing $1B+/day. Thesis: the internet for money needs its own native financial infrastructure, not TradFi ports. On-chain order books, not AMMs, are the future of DeFi.

title: Dollar Decline, AI Bubble & Tail Risk
speaker: Nassim Taleb
contrarian_takes: Current AI leaders may not be long-term winners
Dollar declining as store of value despite transaction dominance
quotable: I'm sure someone's going to make a lot of money in AI, but doesn't necessarily have to be these companies.
summary: Taleb on structural dollar decline, erratic tariff policy, AI bubble risk, and tail hedging. Gold is structurally rising as BRICS central banks accumulate — not a trade call but a structural shift. AI companies face historical pattern: pioneers rarely win (like cars, airlines, PCs). Universa's tail hedging strategy returned >100% annual ROC. Most underpriced risk: large tail drawdowns across all sectors.

title: AI, Education & The Future of Human Thinking
speaker: Po-Shen Loh
contrarian_takes: Competition as motivation is ultimately unsatisfying
quotable: Using AI to do your writing homework is like driving your car one mile for exercise.
summary: Math Olympiad coach on AI and education: humans were top species because of cognitive ability — AI changes that equation. Education needs to shift from knowledge transfer to wisdom cultivation. Mathematical thinking (problem-solving, proof-based reasoning) becomes more important, not less.

title: Tesla Berlin, FSD, Optimus & Future of Work
speaker: Elon Musk
contrarian_takes: Robotics bottleneck is mechanical not software
quotable: Working will be optional. Like long-term, which is 10 years from now or less.
summary: Musk at Giga Berlin: FSD approved Netherlands March 20, 'fall asleep and wake up at destination' by year-end. CyberCab volume production April. Optimus humanoid robot production starting. 5 major production lines launching this year. Tesla Semi going to Europe next year. Battery cell production at Giga Berlin. European auto industry stagnating from lack of innovation and resistance to electrification.

title: Scaling Intelligence, Safety & The AI Tsunami
speaker: Dario Amodei
contrarian_takes: CEO voluntarily constraining own power via governance structure
Deliberately losing commercial races for safety
quotable: It is surprising to me that we are, in my view, so close to these models reaching the level of human intelligence.
summary: Amodei on scaling laws, safety, and Anthropic's founding. Left OpenAI because leadership wasn't serious about safety despite scaling breakthroughs. Scaling laws: compute doubles every 4 months, models approaching human-level intelligence. AI as 'tsunami on the horizon' — public hasn't grasped what's coming. Biology background drove founding vision: AI to solve diseases too complex for humans. Two founding convictions: scaling works + we must get it right.

title: Hard Lessons: Stan Druckenmiller — Invest, Then Investigate
speaker: stan-druckenmiller
key_quote: My advantage is not IQ, it's trigger pulling.
summary: Druckenmiller on investing principles: invest first investigate second, size matters more than being right (Soros lesson), contrarianism overrated (crowd right 80%), 18mo-3yr sweet spot. Technical analysis ~20% as effective as 40yrs ago — crowded signals decay. Courage decays with age/success — better PM in 30s-40s despite less wisdom now. 15+ years of imposter syndrome. Mentors are near-necessary condition. Current: eclectic equities, Japan/Korea, short USD + bonds, long copper/gold, reduced AI. Macro back after 10-15yr drought. Teva trade: investor base gap (6x→12x). Biotech = best AI use case. Nvidia: bought before he could spell it, trusted partner, doubled after ChatGPT, sold at 800 (should have held to 1400).

title: Full Interview: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei on Pentagon Feud
speaker: dario-amodei
key_quote: Disagreeing with the government is the most American thing in the world.
summary: Amodei defends Anthropic's two red lines with Pentagon: no domestic mass surveillance, no fully autonomous weapons. Anthropic was FIRST to deploy on classified cloud, deployed across IC and military. Pentagon gave 3-day ultimatum; designated Anthropic a 'supply chain risk' (normally used against foreign adversaries). Amodei: AI reliability insufficient for autonomous weapons, Congress needs to catch up with technology. Supply chain designation is 'retaliatory and punitive' — unprecedented against an American company.

title: Joe Rogan Experience #2461 - Robert F. Kennedy Jr.
speaker: rfk-jr
key_quote: We were taking the 63 million poorest kids in our country, giving them taxpayer-funded diabetes.
summary: RFK Jr details first year as HHS Secretary: $100B/yr Medicaid fraud (Cuba/Russia/Somalia operations), MFN drug pricing (Americans now pay lowest in developed world), food pyramid overhaul, SNAP reform (20 states ban candy/soda), phone-free schools (26 states), prior authorization elimination, medical records portability, price transparency, glyphosate national security EO, dye removal by year-end, peptide reclassification pending, psychedelic therapy for PTSD under development. Core thesis: chronic disease epidemic is manufactured by perverse incentives — everyone profits from sickness, nobody is incentivized to prevent it.

title: Software Stocks Implode, Claude's Hit List, State of the Union Reactions, Trump's Tariff Pivot
key_quote: We've moved from 'when' to 'if.' The market is debating if these cash flows will even exist.
summary: Claude's 'kill list' of SaaS companies that AI will replace. Software stocks imploding as AI commoditizes features. State of the Union reaction. Tariff pivot analysis. Key debate: is this the SaaS apocalypse or just a correction? Sacks argues AI is deflationary for software; Chamath sees opportunity in the rubble.

title: Perplexity Computer: New Autonomous AI Agent
speaker: julian-goldie
key_quote: The most powerful AI system in 2026 isn't the one built on the best single model. It's the one that can intelligently orchestrate all of them.
summary: Deep dive into Perplexity Computer — autonomous AI agent that can browse, code, and execute multi-step tasks. Demo of real-time web browsing, code execution, and task completion. Comparison with OpenAI's operator. Key: Perplexity positioning as autonomous work assistant, not just search.

title: Cathie Wood on Tesla, Bitcoin, and Whether AI Is in a Bubble
speaker: cathie-wood
key_quote: Walls of worry produce the most durable bull markets.
summary: Wood argues AI is NOT a bubble — unlike dotcom, investors are climbing a wall of worry (most durable bull markets). Competition from China (DeepSeek) is accelerating, not threatening, US innovation. Biggest opportunity: autonomous mobility ($10T in 5-10yr). Tesla target: $2,600 by 2029 (excludes Optimus). Bitcoin: conviction increased, gold is leading indicator. Genomics most undervalued. Wright's Law drives all forecasts. A recession would be a 'coiled spring' for tech adoption.

title: My Multi-Agent Team with OpenClaw
speaker: brian-casel
key_quote: Not do I want a personal assistant, but what if this could fill roles on my team?
summary: Casel demonstrates his multi-agent team built with OpenClaw — autonomous agents handling different business functions. Practical walkthrough of agent orchestration, skill creation, and how a solo founder uses AI agents as a force multiplier.

title: The Next Era of AI is Onchain — Illia Polosukhin at NearCon 2026
speaker: illia-polosukhin
key_quote: Blockchain has always been easier for computers to participate in than humans. It was built for this.
summary: Polosukhin (NEAR co-founder, Transformer co-author) on AI agents living on-chain. Thesis: autonomous AI agents need their own wallets, identity, and economic infrastructure — blockchain is the natural substrate. NEAR building 'chain abstraction' to make this seamless.

title: Inside Robinhood Ventures Fund I: Bill Ackman in Conversation with Vlad Tenev
key_quote: The greatest long-standing inequity in capital markets today.
summary: Robinhood launches Ventures Fund I — closed-end fund giving retail investors access to pre-IPO companies (DataBricks, Stripe, Ramp, Recor, Boom Aerospace, Aura). $350M seed portfolio at cost + $1B raise. Permanent capital vehicle (no LP pressure to distribute). Key insight: companies staying private longer = greatest inequity in capital markets. Retail now 25% of US public equity markets and growing.

title: Act as If Nothing Matters — Alan Watts on Letting Go
speaker: alan-watts
key_quote: Action without anxiety is the truest form of intelligence.
summary: Philosophical meditation on releasing the burden of seriousness. Core thesis: the universe is playful, not purposeful — seriousness is self-inflicted suffering. Acting 'as if nothing matters' is not nihilism but clarity: caring deeply while holding lightly. The child plays, the sage plays — only the anxious adult demands meaning be manufactured. Life is not a problem to be solved but an experience to be lived.

title: On Artificial Intelligence — Naval Ravikant
speaker: naval-ravikant
key_quote: Every human is now a spellcaster. AI is a magic wand handed to every person.
summary: Naval on AI's philosophical implications: intelligence becoming a commodity changes everything about economics, education, and human purpose. Specific knowledge (creativity, judgment, accountability) becomes MORE valuable, not less. AI won't replace humans who can't be replaced — it replaces humans doing replaceable work.

title: Michael Saylor's Strategy World 2026 Keynote: Digital Credit
speaker: michael-saylor
key_quote: Most credit is manufactured to be awful — very tax-inefficient, and it's illegal for you to buy it.
summary: Saylor presents Digital Credit (STRC) — converting Bitcoin into 11%+ yield credit with tax-deferred return-of-capital treatment. Maps capital duration from exchange leverage (1hr) to equity (100yr). Key: programmable credit adaptable to ETFs, tokens, bank accounts. Claims $300T addressable market. Describes reflexive flywheel: credit→amplification→volatility→options→enterprise value→capital.

title: President Trump: Address on Major Combat Operations in Iran
speaker: donald-trump
key_quote: When we are finished, take over your government. It will be yours to take.
summary: Presidential address announcing Operation Midnight Hammer against Iran. Declares US obliterated Iran's nuclear program at Fordow and Isfahan; now targeting missiles, navy, and proxy infrastructure. Offers IRGC 'complete immunity or certain death.' Calls on Iranian people to seize government after operations conclude.

title: The Hidden Reason AI Needs Bitcoin
speaker: jordi-visser
key_quote: AI cannot survive without crypto. It can't survive. And the reason it can't survive is because the fiat system financial guardrails cannot handle the speed of AI.
summary: Visser's thesis: AI needs Bitcoin because autonomous agents need a permissionless, programmable monetary system. AI agents can't open bank accounts. Bitcoin/Lightning is the payments rail for the machine economy. Also: AI deflationary for services but inflationary for energy/commodities.

title: Lyn Alden: Why the Fed WON'T Do a Nuclear Print (And What That Means)
speaker: lyn-alden
key_quote: When you only have a gradual print scenario, asset-specific things matter way more than the macro backdrop.
summary: Alden argues against 'nuclear print' thesis — Fed won't do massive QE because bank cash ratios are still high, no COVID-scale disruption, and a little printing goes a long way. Real risk is societal: top 10% hold 90% of stocks and do 50% of consumer spending. Dividend yields at dotcom-bubble lows. Gold outperforming S&P even with dividends. Gradual print means Bitcoin must compete on its own merits — not a rising tide.

title: What is Perplexity Computer?
speaker: greg-isenberg
key_quote: I'm really interested in the OpenClawification of the internet.
summary: Isenberg's take on Perplexity Computer as startup opportunity detector — AI agents creating new business models. Focus on how entrepreneurs should think about AI-first products. The 'pick and shovel' opportunities in the AI agent era.

title: Ben Horowitz: xAI Executive Exodus, Ilya's $5B SSI Valuation, Apple's AI Crisis, The Pace of AI
speaker: ben-horowitz
key_quote: AI will be a full-out economic actor and it will come from new banks and new money — crypto-based.
summary: Horowitz (a16z) on xAI executive exodus, Ilya's $5B SSI valuation, Apple's AI crisis. AI pace is accelerating — models improving faster than companies can ship products. Apple missed the AI wave and may not recover. Crypto + AI convergence is real: a16z investing at intersection.

title: How to Use AI in 2026: Opendoor Founder's Advice (JD Ross & Joe Lonsdale)
key_quote: Not a single one of our full-time engineers right now writes code anymore.
summary: Lonsdale (8VC) and JD Ross (Opendoor/WithCoverage) on AI in 2026. Zero engineers write code manually — all use Codex/Claude Code/Cursor, one engineer runs 6 concurrent agents. Linear tickets auto-spawn dev environments that oneshot PRs. Enterprise features built and shipped in one day. SaaS barbell: model labs eat top, college kids oneshot the bottom, middle collapses. St. Patrick principle: don't change frameworks, make them better within existing mental models. Jevons Paradox: cheaper engineering = MORE demand for engineers. AI spam crisis will force trust-chain communication. Best startup framework: take what rich people have (20 trusted experts on call) and democratize via AI. We're in a second invention era — equal footing for everyone. Model capability is free R&D — invest in context/data instead. Email is the API of business. WithCoverage: 700+ enterprise customers, profitable from scratch.

title: Why Specialized Agents are Superior (How I Built an OpenClaw Superteam)
speaker: Riley Brown

theses.claim: Narrow agents with 7-10 skills operating in a team outperform mega-agents with 30+ skills
theses.evidence: 200 hours of testing across OpenClaw, Manus, Claude Code, Perplexity Computer

theses.claim: As agent skills increase beyond 7-10, dependability decreases — context gets clouded, wrong tools used, personality jumbles
theses.evidence: Direct testing with his first multi-skill OpenClaw agent

theses.claim: Persistent computer agents (OpenClaw model) will win over per-task computer spin-up (Manus/Perplexity model)
theses.evidence: Argues intent/purpose requires persistence, but acknowledges cloud scaling is unsolved

theses.claim: Agents need intent (specific goals/KPIs) not just prompts — Emmett Shear: 'Prompts are so late 2025'
theses.evidence: His YouTube agent with 3 goals (subs/views/conversions) performs better than general agent

theses.claim: Journal agent pattern: one agent logs everything, other agents read its journal for shared context

title: The design process is dead. Here's what's replacing it.
speaker: Jenny Wen

theses.claim: The classic design process (discover→mock→iterate→ship) is dead — engineering velocity with AI forces design to change from gatekeeper to co-pilot
theses.evidence: Jenny's direct experience leading design at Anthropic where engineers run 7 Claude Code agents simultaneously

theses.claim: Designer time allocation shifted: mocking dropped from 60-70% to 30-40%, replaced by engineer pairing and actual code implementation
AI will get better at taste and judgment — 'we might be holding on to that a little bit too much'
theses.evidence: Observing how fast AI took over coding despite skepticism; someone still needs to be accountable for decisions

theses.claim: Chat/terminal UI isn't going away — talking scales across all intelligence levels
theses.evidence: Kevin Wheel's point + OpenClaw's chat channel innovation

theses.claim: Building trust through speed: ship early, respond publicly, iterate fast — not 10 days to build Co-work, but 10 days from internal prototype to external ship
Three hiring archetypes for the AI era: strong generalist (block-shaped), deep specialist (top 10%), craft new grad (blank slate, eager)

title: Why Specialized Agents are Superior (How I Built an OpenClaw Superteam)
speaker: Riley Brown

theses.claim: Narrow agents with 7-10 skills in teams outperform mega-agents with 30+ skills
Persistent computer agents (OpenClaw) beat per-task spin-up (Manus/Perplexity)
Agents need intent/purpose, not just prompts

title: The design process is dead. Here's what's replacing it.
speaker: Jenny Wen

theses.claim: Classic design process is dead — engineering velocity forces design into co-pilot role
AI will get better at taste and judgment than we expect
Building trust through speed: ship early, respond publicly, iterate fast

title: The Google Maps Trick That Gets Local Businesses 100+ Leads a Month
channel: Noah Igler - Local Service Business SEO
summary: Top 3 Google Maps results get 70%+ of calls. Systematic approach: optimize Google Business Profile, build dedicated service pages, generate reviews, build citations and backlinks. Case studies show 100-286 leads/month results.
theses: Google Maps map pack is the #1 patient acquisition channel for local healthcare
Review volume is the single highest-leverage trust signal
Service page depth directly correlates with relevance ranking

title: The Google Maps Trick That Gets Local Businesses 100+ Leads a Month
speaker: Noah Igler
quotable: If your phone isn't ringing, it's not because you're bad at what you do. It's because you're not showing up top three on Google Maps.
Those top three spots get over 70% of all the phone calls.
This stuff isn't magic. It's just what happens when you show Google the right signals.
summary: Local SEO playbook for getting into Google Maps top 3 results. Three signals: relevance (service pages + GBP optimization), reputation (reviews + citations + backlinks), proximity. Step-by-step implementation: fix GBP, build service pages, generate reviews consistently, build citations and backlinks. Case studies: plumbing company 0→100 leads/month in 5 months, landscaping company hit 286 leads/month.

title: Building a Million Dollar Zero Human Company with OpenClaw
key_thesis: A properly scaffolded OpenClaw can run a real business (not just pump tokens) with zero human employees, making $80K/month at $400/month LLM cost
